title: Question 1.4
statement_text: What is the smallest possible exponent $C_{p,k}$ for which Theorem 1.3 is true?
context_text: The paper proves Theorem 1.3 with exponent $C_{p,k} = \frac{k-2}{c_{p,3}} + 1$, and shows via constructions of $k$-colored sum-free sets that the smallest possible exponent must be at least $\frac{k-2}{c_{p,k}} + 1$. The achieved exponent is within a factor of $O(\log p)$ from this lower bound (independently of $k$), but the exact optimum remains open.
notes: PDF source — math may be garbled; the exponent expressions involve infimum definitions of constants $c_{p,k}$ and $c_{p,3}$ that appear clean in context.
paper_title: A polynomial bound for the arithmetic $k$-cycle removal lemma in vector spaces
